sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with a 3-month history of a cough, which initially started after loading horses and loading hay for 400 bills a day.
The cough has been intermittent and resolved with a recent cold.
The patient has a history of exercise, including biking, and typically does not get sick.
The patient is nervous about the incidentally found lung nodule on their chest X-ray and CT scan.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, which is currently under control with methotrexate 7.5mg weekly.
The patient was a previous smoker, quitting approximately 7 years ago.
Review of systems: The patient denies any pain or shortness of breath.
Current medications: Methotrexate 7.5mg weekly.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's blood pressure is 124/76, heart rate is 70, respiration rate is 16, oxygen saturation on room air is 98%, and the patient is afebrile.
Physical exam: The lungs are clear and equal bilateral, with no wheezes or rales.
The heart is regular rate and rhythm, with no clicks, rubs, or murmurs.
The extremities are pink, warm, and dry, with no edema.
There is slight swelling of the knuckles on the third digit of each hand, which is normal for someone with RA.
Test Results: The chest CT shows a solitary 2cm nodule in the lateral aspect of the right upper lobe, which is smooth in appearance and without evidence of emphysematous disease.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an incidentally found right upper lobe lung nodule.
Problem list: Lung nodule; Rheumatoid Arthritis (under control).
Plan:
I ordered a pulmonary function test (PFT) to establish a baseline for the patient's lung function.
I also scheduled a PET CT to determine if the nodule is metabolically active.
I plan to remove the nodule via video-assisted thoracoscopy, which involves three small incisions on the side of the patient's right chest.
The procedure will be performed under general anesthesia, taking approximately 1.5 hours, and the patient will be admitted for one night with a chest tube until the following day.
The patient will continue to follow up with their rheumatologist and continue their medication therapy as previously outlined.
Follow-up: The patient will see the doctor again in a few minutes to complete paperwork and begin the treatment process.
